1: 'But it displeased Jonah exceedingly, and he was angry' (vayera el-Yonah ra'ah gedolah vayichar lo). Now the narrative delivers the delayed explanation for ch. 1's flight. The prophet's anger is directed not at divine injustice but at divine mercy. The language is extreme: ra'ah gedolah — a great evil/disaster — the same phrase used of the great storm in 1:12 and Nineveh's wickedness in 1:2. For Jonah, YHWH's mercy on Nineveh is as catastrophic as a storm at sea or a city's wickedness. The narrative's irony has been building since ch. 1; here it breaks open.
2: Jonah's prayer reveals his theology and his motivation: 'Is this not what I said when I was yet in my country? That is why I made haste to flee to Tarshish; for I knew that you are a gracious God and merciful, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love, and relenting from disaster.' The theological formula Jonah quotes — El chanun verachum, erech apayim verav chesed venicham al hara'ah — is almost verbatim from Exod 34:6-7, the great YHWH self-disclosure after the golden calf. This is orthodox Israelite theology. Jonah's problem is not ignorance of YHWH's character; he knew it perfectly — and fled precisely because he feared it would apply to Nineveh.
3: 'Therefore now, O YHWH, please take my life from me, for it is better for me to die than to live.' Jonah's death wish echoes Elijah's in 1 Kgs 19:4 ('it is enough; now, O YHWH, take away my life, for I am no better than my fathers'). Both prophets express suicidal ideation after prophetic crisis. Jonah's crisis is not exhaustion or persecution but rather his theological discomfort with YHWH's mercy extending to enemies. He would prefer death to living in a world where Nineveh is spared.
4: YHWH's response is a single question: 'Do you do well to be angry?' (hahetev chara lach). The question is open rather than condemnatory. YHWH does not rebuke; he interrogates. This is the rhetorical strategy of the book's final chapter: instead of declaring Jonah wrong, YHWH draws out the logic of Jonah's position through experience (the plant), then mirrors it back with a final unanswered question (4:11). The divine pedagogy here is experiential and dialogical.
5: Jonah goes outside the city to sit and watch what will happen to it. He still harbors hope that YHWH will destroy Nineveh — or perhaps he cannot accept the repentance's permanence. He makes himself a booth (sukkah) for shade, a temporary shelter that recalls the Feast of Booths (Sukkot) — pilgrimage, dependence, and impermanence. Jonah sits outside a foreign city under his makeshift shelter, watching for a destruction that will not come.
6: YHWH appoints (manna) a plant — the qiqayon, usually identified as the castor-oil plant (Ricinus communis), which can grow very rapidly. The plant grows overnight to provide shade over Jonah's head and relieves his discomfort. Jonah 'was exceedingly glad' (vayismach Yonah al-haqiqayon simchah gedolah) — the first time in the book that Jonah experiences joy, and it is over a plant. His emotional investment in the plant prepares the lesson of vv. 10-11.
7: At dawn the next day, YHWH appoints (manna) a worm (tola'at) that attacks the plant so that it withers. The worm is YHWH's instrument just as the fish was — all of creation is at YHWH's disposal. The reversal is swift: the joy of v. 6 becomes loss in v. 7. Jonah's shade is removed, setting up the conditions for the final object lesson.
8: YHWH appoints (manna) a 'scorching east wind' (ruach qadim charishit) — the sirocco, the desert wind that blows from the east in the Near East and raises temperatures dramatically while lowering humidity. Jonah is exposed, hot, and despairing. Again he wishes to die: 'It is better for me to die than to live.' The parallel with v. 3 is exact: the same death wish, now triggered by the plant's loss rather than Nineveh's salvation. YHWH asks again: 'Do you do well to be angry for the plant?' Jonah doubles down: 'Yes, I do well to be angry, angry enough to die.'
9: Jonah's response — 'Yes, I do well to be angry, angry enough to die' — is the most theologically revealing line he speaks. He has admitted that his anger over the plant is worth dying over. He is more exercised over the loss of a gourd than over the fate of 120,000 people. This is not cruelty but proportionality: Jonah's value system assigns more weight to his personal comfort and his national interests than to Gentile lives. YHWH's final speech uses this admission against him.
10: YHWH's final speech begins: 'You pity (chasta) the plant for which you did not labor, nor did you make it grow, which came into being in a night and perished in a night.' The key verb chus (to pity/spare) is used only here and in v. 11. Jonah has an emotional attachment to the plant; he did nothing to create it; it lasted one night. Yet he grieves its loss. The argument from lesser to greater (qal vachomer) is established: if Jonah can pity a plant he didn't create, how much more should YHWH pity a city?
11: 'And should I not pity (lo achus) Nineveh, that great city, in which there are more than 120,000 persons who do not know their right hand from their left, and also much cattle?' The final question of the book — and it is left deliberately unanswered. '120,000 who do not know their right hand from their left' is typically interpreted as infants or young children (not yet morally responsible), making the population figure much larger. 'And also much cattle' adds a note of absurdist pathos: YHWH's care for livestock mirrors his care for the ignorant. The book ends mid-conversation, Jonah's response unrecorded — the reader must answer YHWH's question for themselves.
